costs: Dual-site administrative and staffing overhead during transition
Legislative amendment cost and negotiation time
Risk of transitional disruption similar to Poland's 2017 double-cohort bottleneck if poorly sequenced (F-live-polish_reform-05)
content.equity_impact: Mixed: benefits younger children's access and commute burden but shifts equity risk to older pupils' consolidated site sorting; requires explicit non-discrimination safeguards given ECtHR rulings on catchment-based segregation (F-live-portuguese_reform-09).
expected_benefits: Preserves youngest children's local schooling, avoiding the longest relative commute burden on the most vulnerable age group
Improves access to specialist teaching for older pupils without requiring full-village closure
Provides a middle path distinct from binary open/close decisions
content.goal: Preserve early-years community access to schooling in the village while consolidating upper primary grades into a larger, better-resourced center school, reducing per-pupil overhead for the highest grades where economies of scale matter most.

implementation_steps.action: Identify tankerület clusters where lower-grade viability differs materially from upper-grade viability and pilot cycle-splitting in 3-5 districts
Establish or designate a regional ISCED2-type consolidated school with guaranteed transport for upper-grade pupils from constituent villages
Amend Public Education Act provisions to permit and standardize partial-cycle village schools as a distinct legal category
Track disruption effects (achievement, attendance) of the transition, learning from Poland's 2017 reversal disruption evidence

mechanisms: Splitting cycles mirrors Poland's 1999 catchment-area consolidation for older pupils while preserving primary buildings, distinct from outright closure (F-live-polish_reform-03).

Retaining only lower grades locally reduces the commute burden for younger children, who are most vulnerable to the documented negative effects of long bus rides (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10).

Consolidating upper grades allows access to specialist teachers, addressing the shortage/aging-workforce problem more efficiently than spreading thin subject specialists across many small schools (F-live-portuguese_reform-04, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-09).

National funding formula minimum class-size thresholds (14-27 pupils) are more feasible to meet at a consolidated upper-grade site than dispersed across villages (F-live-hungarian_education_system-04).
risks: Poland's 2017 experience shows structural reorganization reversed for political rather than evidence reasons can create acute disruption (~7,000 school closures, 10,000 displaced teachers) if implementation is rushed (F-live-polish_reform-04, F-live-polish_reform-05)
Upper-grade consolidation site selection could recreate inter-municipal competition dynamics documented in Hungarian micro-regions (F-live-school_network_planning-09)
Church-sector schools may selectively absorb upper-grade pupils, intensifying segregation given their greater selection freedom (F-live-finnish_reform-09, F-live-portuguese_reform-08)
content.title: Structural grade-splitting: retain lower grades locally, centralize upper grades (Polish-pattern reorganization)
